For the algorithm `fetch_candidate_head`, it is from https://github.com/ethereum/sharding/blob/develop/docs/doc.md#fetch-candidate-heads-in-reverse-sorted-order .
It's idea is
> The idea is that this algorithm is guaranteed to check potential head candidates in highest-to-lowest sorted order of score, with the second priority being oldest to most recent.

I am thinking one example, which follows what you assume above.
Assume we have `batch1 = [1, 2, 3]` and `batch2 = [4, 5]`.
First, `batch1` arrives and we have `new_logs = [1, 2, 3]`.
Before `batch2` arrives, the outcome of `fetch_candidate_head` will be `3`, `2`, `1`. Anytime as long as `batch2` arrives, the result of `fetch_candidate_head` would be the  element in `batch2`, instead of the one in `batch1`.
So I think for this case the algorithm is right.

However, if we assume the `batch1 = [1, 1]` and `batch2 = [2]`. As long as batch2 arrives after `batch1` and before `fetch_candidate_head` is called, the order of the outcome would be `1, 1, 2`, which seems not consistent with the idea of the algorithm "The idea is that this algorithm is guaranteed to check potential head candidates in highest-to-lowest sorted order of score, with the second priority being oldest to most recent.".

This is something that we can consider offering as a standalone op: if people have floating-point images, they can normalize them to uint8 images either by using the functions provided in this gist or by doing something themselves. IMO, the image summary op should not deal with float-to-uint8 normalization. (It would be one thing if there were a canonical transformation that would clearly be what people would usually want, but that's very far from the truth.) This summary op should not do two things.

There's no formal regression here: this API is new, and is not related to `tf.summary.image`. Though the APIs obviously serve the same purpose, we have no obligation to support the same exact contracts—and we already don't; for instance, we drop the `family` parameter. (FWIW, Dandelion suggested that we do make changes like this: that we take this as an opportunity to design *better* APIs instead of tying ourselves to the old ones.)

People who _really_ want float-to-uint8 normalization and _really_ don't want to do so in a separate op for whatever reason can still use the `tf.summary.image` API. We have data compatibility forever.

> Should we also suppress this message in staging mode? Or do we expect people to know about their staging accounts?

I don't have a strong opinion on this either way. If we want to suppress this message when using the staging server, we can simply change this line to `if not config.dry_run` (and fix the test that will break).

> If not, should we note when what we're registering for is a staging account?

If `TOXENV` is set, tox will run that, so these two are equivalent:

```
tox -e $SOMETHING
TOXENV=$SOMETHING tox
```

As far as the python version goes, if you provide an environment that specifies no python (eg: `django11` instead of `py36-django11`), it'll use the default system python (and just that one!) (which you override in each row in the travis matrix).

So, in practice, running `tox` here is the same as the current command.

Here's an example one and its results on travis in case you want to inspect this a bit more:

* https://travis-ci.org/pimutils/todoman
